title: PFC Joseph P. Dwyer Peer Support Program Act
short_title: PFC Joseph P. Dwyer Peer Support Program Act
stage_label: Referred to the Subcommittee on Health.
plain_summary: This bill creates a new program run by the Department of Veterans Affairs called the PFC Joseph P. Dwyer Peer Support Program. The program will give money to organizations that help veterans with mental health problems by connecting them with other veterans who understand what they are going through. These organizations can be nonprofits that already help veterans, official veteran groups, or state and local veteran agencies. The program will have a committee to make sure it follows good rules and standards. Congress is allowing up to twenty-five million dollars to be spent on this program over three years.
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Health.

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the PFC Joseph P. Dwyer Peer Support Program Act.

sections.title: Section 2 PFC Joseph P. Dwyer Peer Support Program
sections.text: The Secretary of Veterans Affairs must create a grant program called the PFC Joseph P. Dwyer Peer Support Program. Through this program, the Secretary will give money to qualified organizations to help them start peer-to-peer mental health programs for veterans. These programs connect veterans with other veterans who can provide support and understanding. Qualified organizations that can apply for grants include nonprofit groups that have helped veterans with mental health in the past, veteran service organizations that Congress has officially recognized, and state, local, or tribal veteran service agencies and their leaders. Each organization that gets a grant can receive up to two hundred and fifty thousand dollars. Organizations must use the grant money to run a program that meets the standards the Secretary creates, hire veterans to work as peer specialists who can meet with other veterans in groups or one-on-one to provide support that is not medical treatment, provide mental health support to veterans twenty-four hours a day and seven days a week, and hire staff to help run the program. The Secretary must create an advisory committee to develop proper standards for these programs. The standards must include training requirements for veteran volunteers who provide peer support both when they start and on an ongoing basis, rules about how many staff members are needed to run the programs, and best practices for helping each veteran who uses the services. The Secretary cannot require organizations that receive grants to keep records about which veterans seek help or to report any personal information about these veterans back to the Secretary. Congress authorizes spending up to twenty-five million dollars to fund this program during the three years after this law goes into effect.
